notes: Ancient Greek poet traditionally dated to the 7th century BCE, known for works like Theogony and Works and Days.

docs.title: Letter 54
docs.author: Julian the Apostate
docs.subject_summary: The wise Hesiod4 thinks that we ought to invite our neighbours to our feasts that they may rejoice
docs.excerpt: The wise Hesiod recommends that we invite our neighbors to our feasts, since they share in our sorrows and should share in our joys. But I say we should invite our friends rather than our neighbors — because it is possible to have a neighbor who is an enemy, but for a friend to be

docs.title: Letter 129
docs.author: Synesius of Cyrene
docs.excerpt: … pieces, and to our friend Proclus — as noble Hesiod prescribes — a third more than he lent me. He lent me sixty; the note said seventy; I am sending eighty. He would have had more if my first shipment had reached you.
